mission.title: Install and Configure Prometheus on Kubernetes
mission.description: Prometheus is a powerful monitoring system and time series database that collects metrics from configured targets, evaluates rule expressions, and triggers alerts based on specified conditions. Installing Prometheus allows you to monitor your applications and infrastructure effectively.

steps.title: Add the Prometheus Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Prometheus Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration:
```bash
helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Prometheus using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install Prometheus in a new namespace called 'monitoring':
```bash
helm install prometheus prometheus-community/prometheus --namespace monitoring --create-namespace --version 14.0.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Prometheus pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace monitoring
```

steps.title: Access the Prometheus UI
steps.description:
To access the Prometheus UI, set up port forwarding:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/prometheus-server --namespace monitoring 9090:80
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
You may want to configure resource limits for the Prometheus deployment. Edit the values.yaml file or use the following command to set resource limits:
```bash
helm upgrade prometheus prometheus-community/prometheus --namespace monitoring --set server.resources.limits.cpu=500m --set server.resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have the Prometheus server running in the 'monitoring' namespace, and you will be able to access the Prometheus UI at http://localhost:9090. Ensure that the pods are in the 'Running' state.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
helm upgrade prometheus prometheus-community/prometheus --namespace monitoring --set server.resources.limits.cpu=500m --set server.resources.limits.memory=512Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Prometheus release, run the following command to uninstall it from the 'monitoring' namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall prometheus --namespace monitoring
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
After uninstalling the release, remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd prometheuses.monitoring.coreos.com
kubectl delete crd prometheusrules.monitoring.coreos.com
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace monitoring
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the 'monitoring' namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace monitoring
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of your Prometheus installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace monitoring -o yaml > prometheus-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update your Helm repository and then upgrade the Prometheus release to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade prometheus prometheus-community/prometheus --namespace monitoring --version 15.0.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, check that the Prometheus pods are running with the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace monitoring
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you see that the Prometheus pods are in a 'CrashLoopBackOff' state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace monitoring
```
Look for any configuration issues or resource limits that might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Prometheus UI, ensure that the service is running:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace monitoring
```
If the service is not running, check the pod status and logs for issues.

troubleshooting.title: High memory usage
troubleshooting.description:
If Prometheus is consuming too much memory, consider adjusting the resource limits. You can check the current resource usage with:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace monitoring
```
Then, update the resource limits as needed.

troubleshooting.title: Alerts not firing
troubleshooting.description:
If alerts are not firing as expected, verify the alerting rules:
```bash
kubectl get prometheusrules --namespace monitoring
```
Check the rules for any misconfigurations or errors in the alert definitions.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster and the Helm and kubectl command-line tools installed.
